architecture.summary: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) consist of an encoder that transforms LLM activation vectors into a sparse, higher-dimensional latent space, and a decoder that projects latents back to the original space. Both are trained jointly to minimize reconstruction error while maintaining sparsity. The paper measures feature stability by training multiple SAEs on identical data with different random seeds, then applying the Hungarian algorithm to compute optimal bijective matchings of latents between independently trained SAEs based on cosine similarity of encoder and decoder vectors.

biblio.title: Sparse Autoencoders Trained on the Same Data Learn Different Features

claims.statement: In an SAE with 131K latents trained on a feedforward network in Llama 3 8B, only 30% of the features were shared across different seeds
For a 32K latent SAE trained on the sixth MLP of Pythia 160M, only 42% of latents are shared across two independently trained SAEs

claims.metric: fraction of unique latents across nine seeds
claims.statement: When training nine independent SAEs with different seeds, the number of latents found in only one SAE is reduced to about 35%

ReLU SAEs trained with the L1 sparsity loss showed greater stability across seeds compared to SAEs using the state-of-the-art TopK activation function, which were more seed-dependent, even when controlling for the level of sparsity
Increasing the number of SAE latents decreases the overlap between different seeds
Latents that most frequently fire in the base SAE are the ones that are shared with all eight other SAE seeds, while those that infrequently fire are not shared with any other SAE
Orphan features (found in only one SAE seed) are often quite interpretable, with a significant fraction of latents found only on one SAE having high-scoring explanations
The set of features uncovered by an SAE should be viewed as a pragmatically useful decomposition of activation space, rather than an exhaustive and universal list of features truly used by the model
